text: The surgeon identifies the anatomical landmarks including the inner iliac artery, external iliac artery, and internal iliac artery, in proximity to the uterus during the myomectomy procedure.

The surgeon opens the surgical field, revealing the deep uterine vein. On the right side, the anterior ligament of the gluteus medius is visible, along with the gluteus medius muscle. The surgeon manipulates the anterior ligament of the gluteus medius, demonstrating the anatomical spaces and occasionally encountering the umbilical artery.

The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and notes its position relative to the uterine artery, which is located behind it. The ureter is visible in the surgical field, with the surgeon pointing out its location multiple times.

The surgeon increases the space in the surgical field to improve visibility, focusing on the area in front of the ureter. The ureter is identified and manipulated, while the pararectal space is opened for further access.
The surgeon grasps the paralytic force and stretches the surrounding tissue, revealing the unstretched fabric. The ester is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies and separates an artery from a vein, possibly the superior physical artery or rectal artery, within the surgical field.

The surgeon manipulates two anatomical structures, preparing to lift the uterus and make an incision that encompasses two-thirds of the fibroid's volume. The fibroid appears disjointed, and the surgeon adjusts the view for better visualization.
The surgeon gently pulls on the tissue, ensuring not to apply excessive tension to avoid breaking. The tissue appears ischemic, indicated by its whitish color, suggesting effective tightening.
